turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We will hear arguments next in New York v. Belton. Mr. Harvey, I think you may proceed whenever you're ready.

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case involves the legality of a search conducted of Appeals which in a divided opinion reversed the appellate court, lower appellate court, and held that the officer had in effect reduced the jacket to his exclusive control when he, if you will, picked it up, and therefore a warrant was at that time necessary under the law. The facts in this case, if I could very briefly run through them -- I think they're very important, and they're pret ty much — well, they're not in dispute by either counsel for the respondent or myself, but I’d like to go through, and start out by or burned marijuana cigarettes in the ashtray of the front por­ tion of the vehicle. At that point in time he again patted down or searched further all of the four occupants of the vehicle, read them their Miranda rights, placed them under arrest for possession of marijuana. At this time we had four defendants outside of the motor vehicle there on the expressway. The officer then went back into the vehicle and on the back seat there were lying five jackets. each and every one of the occupants under arrest for possession of cocaine, obviously a more serious offense. And the defen­ dant --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: At trial did they disclaim any ownership? Or any move to suppress?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Okay, Your Honor, in the jacket, in -- the defendant in this case, the Respondent, now Belton — he had an ID card. I believe it was either a Blue Cross or Blue Shield card with his name on it. And I handled the case at the grand jury, and each of the other occupants pointed the finger or destructible evidence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was this trooper always alone with the four 7

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. He was alone at the scene. There were no other officers present. It was he and the four occupants of the motor vehicle.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did he secure them in any way when they were standing —

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: No, Your Honor, they were in no way secured.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did you say it was a two-door vehicle?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then did he have to get in it and —

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Yes. Your Honor, he had to bend, push the seat to get into the back seat, push the front seat forward, and bend in. At this point in time he was certainly indisposed to a certain extent in that particular area.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I take it any of the four, had he been able to reach it, would have had to get in the same way, would he? of that officer at the vehicle. I think that this is quite frankly absurd. I think, at this point in time, the exclusive control, the Court of Appeals held, of that whole scene -- in other words, now the scene is stabilized,: as far as the majority of the court was concerned, once he placed the individuals under arrest and of course ordered them out of the vehicle. I think that's not realistic —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Were any of them armed?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: There was no evidence in this particular case of any weapons. There were no weapons, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Gone? He could have just' picked up the phone right there in his car. HR. HARVEY: He's outside of his vehicle at this point in time, Your Honor. He could have called initially when he pulled the vehicle over but -- Your Honor, that existed which they are not supposed to -- not authorized to do under state law? I would say, yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We do?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's the federal issue?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: We're dealing here with the constitu­ tionality, Your Honor, of the particular search. That's correct

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They merely found us a new set of facts which the dissenting judge said was wrong, but is that something we can reexamine?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Incicentally, did the officer get them to the police station in his -- ?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Ultimately he did, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He put the four of them in -- what? — his car?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: In his vehicle, that's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And drove them all -- ?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Took them to the police station, and ul­ timately to the magistrate, in that particular area, that's cor­ rect , Your Honor. This Court in Robinson stated that it's not ing on the plain view doctrine, are you, that evidence was in plain view?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're referring to the Chimel case.

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Right, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's a plain view case.

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: In the area of the arrestee; that's cor­ rect. That set forth the area to be searched. I'm taking it certainly a step further, Your Honor, in saying that the area from which a particular defendant or accused person, arrestee, may obtain a weapon or contraband, certainly should apply in this particular case to anything that's in that area reachable by this defendant to, should we say, assault the officer or destroy the evidence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But after he got ahold of it, the occupant couldn't have gotten it, could he? in time wrestled or taken it from him.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So he had a right to search him? MR. HARVEY That’s correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He had a right to search him. MR. HARVEY Incident to the arrest. We're still in a fluid'-situation as the dissent in the court below said at this point

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: While you're interrupted, where was the clear view of this cocaine? MR. HARVEY Pardon me, Your Honor?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The clear view of the cocaine?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Maybe in New York a speeding offsnse would not support a custodial arrest.

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Your Honor, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's your answer then.

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Fine. At that point in time there would be no authority to search. Now, once the marijuana, of course, which was kind of contemporaneous to the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Assume that it was a sufficiently reckless driving and maybe he smelled alcohol on the breath of the driver,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, now --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He could have a right to search.

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: He could have a right, anything that looked like it may contain or could contain --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The difference between search and pat down, I'm talking about.

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Certainly, pat down.

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Yes. We're asking, Your Honors, if you will, the Court to set down a standard or guideline, if you will, in these type of cases, that is, the search incident to lawful arrest cases. There has been a lot of different holdings in the state courts, some commentators have looked at the area o f search incident to lawful arrest and come forward with certain criteria that we think ought to apply. That is -- and be con­ well as by the courts. We would like to see this come from this consideration of individualized factors in that situation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Anytime you've got the custodial arrest, that's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that if this jacket, if he had had this jacket on, it would have been searchable under Robinson?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: No question about that, Your Honor, cer­ tainly. And taking it off, should another rule apply?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or even carrying it in his hand?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: If he were carrying it in his hand, clearly, it would have been searchable under Robinson. If he were seated at a park bench and arrested for a crime that were

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Your Honor, is the position that I take. The area of the arrestee. And in this case, of course, the facts in this case, all the individuals, not just the driver, were of course under arrest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand. For marijuana?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. The loca­ tion -- another criteria -- the location of the arrestees in relation to the automobile and the ease or difficulty to be criteria, as many guidelines as possible, so that he can do his job properly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How can you have a bright line when you are dealing with the word "reasonable," if bright line means a per se rule that's easy for anyone to apply? HR. HARVEY: It's got to, Your Honor, apply; the cir­ cumstances certainly govern what's reasonable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But here you're dealing with basically two separate doctrines, aren't you, the Robinson doctrine of

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the plastic bag is involved in Belton, I mean, -as in Robbins?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Robbins..

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How about the jacket?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: In the trunk, Your Honor, or in the -- ?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You wouldn't argue incident to arrest there, would you?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And so then it would be a —

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Right. In my particular -- it's our po­ sition it doesn't, hut it certainly was unzippered, but it was not visible as a white powder substance without going into the jacket; certainly, Your Honor. Now, in the respondent's brief, he relies heavily on Chadwick -- of the pockets of that same jacket.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So any question about ownership, you would say, is resolved by that ID card?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: There's no question about that, who owned the jacket, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Just as though he had his name on it, as some people do. Your Honor. The respondent relies heavily on Chadwick and Respondent has asserted in his brief that we wholly disagree with the arguments of the state, and I just wanted to begin by making the point that we do not disagree with the state in its arguments that this was within the Chimel scope, focusing on the facts of the case and the reasonableness of the officer's actions under the particular facts of the case. We think that, however, that that kind of inquiry which is amply — the state's position is amply supported, if it be made in this case, is not an inquiry that needs to be made. Robinson and that scope is broad indeed. This case concerns the aspect of a search incident doctrine involving a search of containers or items of some kind that are nearby but not on the person or being worn by the ar­ rested individual. And our position is that such nearby items like the items on the person should be subject to the same test for a valid search incident to arrest as long as the search is with respect to a separate container that may have been seized, seat, and they have probable cause to arrest?

turns.name: FREY
turns.text: Well, the questions that I would ask are at the time the purse is seized, is it within the area where the woman who has been arrested could possibly lunge for it and grab it?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Supposing that it is, you would say, they may search it?

turns.name: FREY
turns.text: If it's still during the arrest process. If it's been put in the back of the patrol car and then they come back and start --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But what if it were just zippered? Draper?

turns.name: FREY
turns.text: 'I think the question the Court has to answer in this situation is whether it is something to which somebody might possibly have gained access during the course of the arrest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We've .certainly sustained as an incident search, a search of a zippered bag, in Draper, Draper.

turns.name: FREY
turns.text: Yes, I would say a zippered contained would be searchable incident to arrest; yes. ButT would have difficulty passenger compartment, is under Adams v. Williams, would have been in the scope of search and seizure.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We'll resume there at one o'clock.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Frey, you may continue,

turns.name: FREY
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. I think when the Court recessed I was discussing the spatial limitations, the limitations of the Chimel test. And it is our view, and I in this Court, Williams had been arrested. The officer then waited until additional officers arrived on the scene, at which point the car was searched. Now, it was not a subject of analy­ sis in the Court's opinion, but the Court did uphold the search of the car and the seizure of the heroin found in the car as incident to Williams' arrest. The same is true in Draper where, I assume,at the time the zipper bag was searched it was within the exclusive control of the officer in this sense. And the problem with the exclu­ sive control test, the way it's being used, is that it essen­ don't think it's any inconsistency in this Court's treatment of the subject that has prompted that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there was no claim in Chadwick, was there, that that was a search incidental to arrest?

turns.name: FREY
turns.text: We did not make that claim; no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I didn't think so. That wasn't an issue before the Court.

turns.name: FREY
turns.text: No, but it was a point that was discussed in the Court's opinion, and in just a second I will get to what lurking in all custodial arrests make warrantless searches of items within the immediate control area reasonable without re­ quiring the arresting officer to calculate the probability of weapons --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I take it you wouldn't have suggested that in Chadwick that footlocker could have been searched if the arrest had taken place in the station, while they were sitting on — right after' the dog had decided what was in the -- ?

turns.name: FREY
turns.text: If the arrest had taken place in the sta­ of the officer. The officer could have taken it and put it away somewhere out of the reach of the arrested individual. There­ fore it seems to me that once the Court has rejected that with respect to items removed from the person of the individual, there is precious little logical basis for treating it differ­ ently if the. item is found nearby. Earlier, during my brother's argument, the question came up about whether he had been wearing a jacket, carrying a jacket; had it down on the park bench, or so on. Of course,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: , You think that you would be making the argument you would be making now if when they arrested the people, just as they were, while the footlocker was in the station-wagon, that they could have searched the footlocker in­ cident to arrest except for the fact it was locked?

turns.name: FREY
turns.text: Except for the lock. Except for the lock­ ing. And I wanted to make the point that there is talk about the privacy interest, and the high privacy interest in the pockets of the jacket and so on. And I think when we are deal­ does not matter as long as at the time it was seized it's still within the Chimel geographical scope of the area within which a search incident __is permissible.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If -- I suppose what you're driving at is that, conceivably, the officer might have said to the four of lengths ahead of me and go to the station? policeman, in the same car.

turns.name: FREY
turns.text: I'm not sure. I don't remember from my reading of the record -- QUESTION Well, that's what -- that's what the lawyer who tried the case said. MR. FREY Well, but it's -- I don't think he put the cocaine in any place where Mr. Belton could reach it. QUESTION Well, they were all in the same car. MR. FREY Well, I -- but if he knew that -- QUESTION Weren't they? Weren't they in the same car? I would like to begin with what I think is a critical point. Chief Judge Cooke, below, for the court of appeals, very clearly pointed out that at no time, at any time in this record was there proof that these materials were within the grabbable reach of these individuals. There was never a finding to that effect by the trial court, there was never a finding to that effect by the appellate division —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean, after they got out of the car?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. After they get out of nothing that could possibly support that exception because in fact they were outside the car?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: In fact, they were outside the car --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, is that the way you read your couht of appeals? was asked of him he was quick to respond, as the record shows, there was just no need for that at any time. He had certainly separated them, as he indicated, he had patted them down, he had searched them totally, and then he went back into the car.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How about the possibility of them not with weapons but simply with their own bodies overpowering him while he was looking in the back seat?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: I think that we have to deal with the record the way it exists. And under the record the way it exists would be able to go into, let's say, the entire room. Or if it were in one room of the house, maybe all the other rooms, they could bolt out the door and go into the other rooms. So there' fore we ought to be able to search all of the rooms in the house. I don't think we've' ever had that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But Chimelwas at least a one-on-one situation, not a four-on-one situation. a factor for consideration. But if I might, if we were to

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Well, because I believe that under South Dakota v. Opperman and the other cases around the car exception, that those areas traditionally do not enjoy the expectation of privacy that private receptacles do. That's what the court said in Opperman. So that if we're talking about, specifically, Opperman, the glove compartment or the trunk or the common areas of the car, the car, since it's highly regulated, since it is an area with windows and so on, does not enjoy the same expecta­ tion of privacy as their''private garments.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: I'm not sure if they would, and it would depend upon —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what good would it do them to make an inventory search if they didn't look in the pockets?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: My recollection of Opperman is that it depends upon their local rules and regulations as to what they can do and what they can't do in the inventory situation. Now, in this case, I think that it bears emphasis that the officer did exactly what this Court indicated in Sanders and in Chadwick i make the difference as it did not in Sanders.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I know, I know, but ' in this case you would still have the problem of proving probable cause, if you went for a warrant.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: No question about that, that there would be the argument made. But even if there were, assuming areas of the car.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Cambria, you wouldn't -- if he had patted down this jacket, which he did, and he had discovered something that it felt like a gun, you still wouldn't suggest that he could search it, would you?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Not under the -- no, for two reasons; no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, yes. Well, you'd make the same argu­ ment you're making now: if you want to get into the jacket, you go get a warrant.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: That question, which certainly is not here at this point --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I don't know. Draper said that that was a good search incident to arrest.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Yes. I understand that. I would say this. First of all.--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: On your argument, could the outcome in Draper be the same? Once the officer arrests a man and takes the bag from him and he's got it in his hands?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Yes -- in my argument it was never taken: from any of the defendants. He had it in his hand without the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He walks out of the car with it on his arm and the officer takes it from him. How does that differ from the case that was taken from Draper?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: I would submit this, that Draper may be qualified by Chadwick and Sanders; it may be, because of the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, well, frankly, I thought what you were arguing was the difference between, distinction between Draper and this case and between the hypothetical my brother White has just given you, and this case; is that they were not within Draper?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: No, because I think in this case the court below, the decision below was, that at no time were these materials within the Chimel standard. They talk of Chimel, talk about Chimel, and specifically distinguish their decision from a case called People v. DeSantis. People v. DeSantis was a Draper case where a briefcase was in the hand of an individual and taken from him at that time. And the court of appeals made it quite clear, below, we are not talking about that, we are talking about the facts before us, which are that there's never any immediate association between the jackets and the individ­ uals after the arrests, so --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you suggest that there's no need to de­

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: i think that that

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Wouldn't that be Draper?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: No. The difference in Draper is that they had probable cause there for the particular item involved. They were told ahead of time that this briefcase that Draper was carrying was where the narcotics were, and there was a specific situation and probable cause which we don't even have here. Assuming we did, that would be Draper, except there's one other fact in our case that's not present in Draper --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the issue in this case is bottomed upon the premise that the arrest was a lawful custodial arrest.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: That's -- yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is it not?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Yes. search and seizure were within the permissible scope of a search there was a mention of plain view.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Plain view comes first. You don't know whether it's within reach, grabbable or otherwise, until you see it.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: No, I might indicate that in Chimel the opinion covered closed drawers, open drawers; plain view, not in plain view; many areas. What I'm suggesting is that if we were to adopt the government's side of this, we would be extinguishing' Chimel, because we wouldn't have to concern our­ selves anymore with whether or not there was a grabbable reach

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: I don't think so at all here; no, not in this particular --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There is no question, at least by hypothe­ sis, that this was a lawful custodial arrest?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the only issue is whether or not this is within the permissible geographic scope of such a search —

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Yes, and I —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -y and seizure.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: What I am suggesting is that we should not -- and the Court has not, in the past -- adopted a place possession; take it to a magistrate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the issue is whether or not this was within the Chimel confines. That's the only issue before us.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Yes. And I think that that is true unless we were to attempt to argue the Government's version, which is, forget about Chimel.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Cambria, may I ask you a question?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Yes, sir, Justice Powell.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: - If this jacket had been on the seat beside were placed under arrest and again, recognizing the two bases for Chimel, was there a basis for believing destruction of evi­ dence or a danger to the officer? And was that manifested by the jacket being within the grabbable reach of the arrestee? That was the actual issue. Because the Court has never said that for any type of arrest, when it's not within the Chimel reach, that you can simply search the entire area, search all of the possessions, or even the car, for that matter. So what I'm indicating is,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What do you do with our Edwards case then, which said that although the search took place an hour and a half after the arrest or two hours after the arrest, since it could have been done at the time of the arrest, it was good at two hours afterwards?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: I think that there is a vast difference between Edwards and this case, because in Mr. Edwards' situation we're talking about his clothes, which were totally immediately associated with him because he was wearing them. There was, therefore, a great jeopardy that the paint flakes which were it was always in danger of having whatever evidence was con­ tained on it being destroyed. So it was a contemporaneous search, no matter what had happened, until you separated the individual from the clothing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, so then it makes no difference when the person is arrested?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: No, it would make a difference under indicated, maybe with regard to these facts the officer had the jacket, readily admitted it, at all times.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Cambria, maybe this is too trivial a question, but you have used the term "grabbable reach" repeat­ edly.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is a grabbable reach a greater or a lesser area than a just plain reach?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Well, I think that grabbable reach, in this case that it was within the grabbable reach. It was never mentioned at one time by any court that it was within the grabbable reach.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but that’s not — in a sense, a con­ stitutional fact, if we're talking about a Fourth Amendment claim. If there was no finding, we are entitled to make our judgment on that. ME.. CAMBRIA: Well, I think that — it seems to me that in this particular case you couldn't make a judgment of majority opinion in talking of certain things mentioned in dis­ sents, when you say, we have in essence construed the record and we find no proof whatsoever that this is within the grabbable reach, I think that's a finding of fact. Because on the other hand, if they would have found to the contrary, then I feel they would have been bound by Chimel, and they have discussed Chimel, clearly, and said that it simply did not come within the confine of Chimel. I think it is most certainly a finding of fact in this case. areas being searched, and certainly not packages which are with­ in the car itself.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Cambria, when you -- as I understand the facts here, after the officer stopped the car, the driver rolled down the left window of the car, and the officer when he went into the back seat was on one side of the car and the four individuals were on the other side. Were they on the left side or the right side of the car?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: No, the only testimony here is that he took the individuals out to the rear of the car, that he first himself hack into the car.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought the four individuals were sepa­ rated from one another.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: That's so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are they all behind the car?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: That's what the record states, Your Honor, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Cambria, let me go back to the footnote that you have referred to in Chief Judge Cooke's opinion, at exclusive control of the officer and it was not within the grab- bable reach of the defendants, then it doesn't fit within the Chimel doctrine. And I think that's exactly what that footnote stands for, by saying, we have it in the police officer's posses sion which, under Chadwick and Sanders, he should therefore take it down to and have the magistrate examine it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: , So you agree with the United States, I gather, if the court of appeals, if the opinion below is read as establishing a control test, you don't defend that? incident to a lawful custodial arrest, did it?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: It was discussed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It was discussed in Chadwick.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the findings were made -- and discussed in a footnote, I guess, in Sanders?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Yes. was made in Sanders that the argument could not have been not -- they did not, they do and they don't -- they do from the standpoint of the fact that it was a consideration which was available, it certainly gave pause to this Court —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The argument was not made in either case.

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: I submit that it wasn't made because it wasn't a valid argument to be made.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the opinions of the court indicate that the various arguments --

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: That it was not a valid argument. New York, was it?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: No, it was not raised, and it finds no support in the record. One of the clarifications I might make is that it was after the time of the discovery of the cocaine that the officer then said, whose jacket is this? And that's when there was a disclaimer. But he had already conducted his search prior to the time he elicited —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Search of what?

turns.name: CAMBRIA
turns.text: Search of the jacket itself. The only point I would make is that the Chimel cri­ teria as set forth by the Court defines the area as the area that the arrestee might reach in order to grab a weapon or evi­ dentiary material. And I think that's here, certainly, in this case on the record on certainly one of two theories. Either the overpowering theory, and I think quite frankly that's the theory that's here clearly on the record below —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If we take your view, I take it that any container in a car, any piece of evidence in a car, or in the yes, Your Honor. Thank you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, wait a minute, you’ve said, under the overpowering theory, or you said, under --

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, also they could have reached in through the --.that it's tied in.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The overpowering theory would depend, I sup pose, if I’understand your shorthand for the theory, upon how many arrestees there were and how many policemen there were arresting them, and which ones were -- • /

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but I mean, that's a danger of being overpowered? You left four of them back there all by them­ selves.

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: In fact --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you got in the car and got back out, and nothing happens.

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: In fact, the —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then you've got.to worry about overpowering ?

turns.name: HARVEY
turns.text: In fact, the officer was not overpowered in this particular case, but he certainly, under the Chimel - Robinson rules had the opportunity and the right to search the CERTIFICATE North American Reporting hereby certifies that the attached pages represent an accurate transcript of electronic sound recording of the oral argument before the Supreme Court of the United States in the matter of: No. 80-328 NEW YORK V. ROGER BELTON proceedings for the records of the Court. BY: CiU sx
